channel.title: Protein Design Podcast
channel.description: Daily automated digest of protein design, antibody engineering & enzyme design research
channel.itunes:author: Eva Dai
channel.itunes:summary: Daily automated digest of protein design, antibody engineering & enzyme design research

item.title: Daily Podcast 2026-03-15
item.description: The biggest shift today comes from a paper on deep brain stimulation that challenges how we think about predicting treatment outcomes. | Another major insight comes from the calcium imaging analysis paper. | The microbiome classification paper reveals something equally surprising about how we visualize biological data.
item.itunes:author: Eva Dai
item.itunes:summary: The biggest shift today comes from a paper on deep brain stimulation that challenges how we think about predicting treatment outcomes. | Another major insight comes from the calcium imaging analysis paper. | The microbiome classification paper reveals something equally surprising about how we visualize biological data.

item.title: Daily Podcast 2026-03-14
item.description: Let me walk you through what actually mattered from today's protein design papers. | Starting with the most conceptually ambitious: Bonnie Berger's group has reframed evolution itself as a constrained diffusion process. | The AlphaFind v2 paper addresses a practical bottleneck that's been holding back structural biology.
item.itunes:author: Eva Dai
item.itunes:summary: Let me walk you through what actually mattered from today's protein design papers. | Starting with the most conceptually ambitious: Bonnie Berger's group has reframed evolution itself as a constrained diffusion process. | The AlphaFind v2 paper addresses a practical bottleneck that's been holding back structural biology.

item.title: Daily Podcast 2026-03-08
item.description: In 2024, bioarchaeologists made a breakthrough in medieval forensics when they identified the remains of a murdered 14th-century nobleman using a combination of DNA analysis, radiocarbon dating, and historical records. | The team extracted mitochondrial DNA from the bones and compared it to living descendants of the noble family. | This identification was significant because it demonstrated that modern forensic techniques could solve medieval cold cases with reasonable certainty.
item.itunes:author: Eva Dai
item.itunes:summary: In 2024, bioarchaeologists made a breakthrough in medieval forensics when they identified the remains of a murdered 14th-century nobleman using a combination of DNA analysis, radiocarbon dating, and historical records. | The team extracted mitochondrial DNA from the bones and compared it to living descendants of the noble family. | This identification was significant because it demonstrated that modern forensic techniques could solve medieval cold cases with reasonable certainty.

item.title: Daily Podcast 2026-03-07
item.description: Derek Lowe at In the Pipeline reports on a biotech startup called Dupeless that's rethinking drug discovery by focusing on "undruggable" targets. | Researchers have developed a rapid on-site detection method for identifying acetaminophen adulteration in herbal tea using gold nanoparticle immunochromatography. | The ability to predict the three-dimensional structure of a protein from its amino acid sequence has potential to provide insights into its function, and in the context of disease, its pathogenic mechanisms and potential drug targets.
item.itunes:author: Eva Dai
item.itunes:summary: Derek Lowe at In the Pipeline reports on a biotech startup called Dupeless that's rethinking drug discovery by focusing on "undruggable" targets. | Researchers have developed a rapid on-site detection method for identifying acetaminophen adulteration in herbal tea using gold nanoparticle immunochromatography. | The ability to predict the three-dimensional structure of a protein from its amino acid sequence has potential to provide insights into its function, and in the context of disease, its pathogenic mechanisms and potential drug targets.

item.title: Daily Podcast 2026-03-02
item.description: When ruminants shift from a forage-based diet to a high-grain diet, the rumen environment changes dramatically. | A new study published in the Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology tracked how the rumen papillae—the finger-like projections in the rumen wall that absorb nutrients—respond at the molecular level during this dietary transition. | What they found was striking: the adaptation process isn't a single, uniform response.
item.itunes:author: Eva Dai
item.itunes:summary: When ruminants shift from a forage-based diet to a high-grain diet, the rumen environment changes dramatically. | A new study published in the Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology tracked how the rumen papillae—the finger-like projections in the rumen wall that absorb nutrients—respond at the molecular level during this dietary transition. | What they found was striking: the adaptation process isn't a single, uniform response.

item.title: Daily Podcast 2026-03-01
item.description: Coral reefs are often celebrated for their colorful fish and intricate structures, but beneath the surface lies an even more complex world: the microbial communities living inside and around corals. | The research team collected coral fragments and seawater samples from 99 reefs across 32 islands in the Pacific Ocean. | By combining this new data with publicly available coral and sponge microbiome datasets, the researchers built what they call the Reef Microbiome Database, or RMD.
item.itunes:author: Eva Dai
item.itunes:summary: Coral reefs are often celebrated for their colorful fish and intricate structures, but beneath the surface lies an even more complex world: the microbial communities living inside and around corals. | The research team collected coral fragments and seawater samples from 99 reefs across 32 islands in the Pacific Ocean. | By combining this new data with publicly available coral and sponge microbiome datasets, the researchers built what they call the Reef Microbiome Database, or RMD.
